Title: Glutamate 1-semialdehyde aminotransferase is connected to GluTR by GluTR-binding protein and contributes to the rate-limiting step of 5-aminolevulinic acid synthesis

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: GluTR-binding protein; scaffold-like ALA synthesis complex component
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: ALA is the universal precursor of tetrapyrroles required for chlorophyll and heme biosynthesis; chlorophyll supports photosynthesis
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Protein accumulates in stromal and membrane fractions; elevated in gsa2
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Short-day and continuous-light conditions; dark incubation with exogenous ALA affects associated protein abundance
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: BiFC; pull-down from plastid lysates; in vitro pull-down; cross-linking MS; BN-PAGE co-migration; MST; gbp knockout comparison
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Facilitates/stabilizes association of GSAAT with GluTR in a tripartite ALA-synthesizing complex, thereby supporting efficient ALA synthesis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GBP interacts strongly with GluTR and also with GSAAT. 2. GBP mediates stable association between GluTR and GSAAT as a scaffold in a tripartite complex. 3. In GBP knockout extracts, GluTR-associated GSAAT is strongly reduced, supporting an intermediary role.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential engineering lever for improving efficiency/stability of the ALA-synthesizing complex, though gbp mutants reportedly show only small ALA-rate reductions and context dependence should be considered
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Effects on flux may be modest in some gbp alleles; excessive manipulation could perturb GluTR/heme homeostasis
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Cross-linking proteomics/MS; ProteomeXchange and jPOST: JPST001362
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Classified as Common Gene because it acts primarily as a scaffold/interaction mediator rather than enzyme or transcriptional regulator; strongest measured affinity is GBP-GluTR (KD 42 nM), then GBP-GSAAT (178 nM), and GBP/GluTR-GSAAT (940 nM).

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: ALA is the universal precursor of tetrapyrroles required for chlorophyll and heme biosynthesis; chlorophyll supports photosynthesis
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Dominant GluTR isoform in leaves; transcripts peak around 2 h after onset of illumination
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Protein most abundant 4-6 h after dark-to-light transition; present in stromal and membrane fractions
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Light regime; dark incubation with exogenous ALA/heme-related feedback strongly affects stability and membrane association
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Immunoblotting; subplastid fractionation; BiFC; pull-down; BN-PAGE; cross-linking MS; MST; ALA feeding
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: GluTR abundance and active soluble fraction are modulated by GSAAT content; in gsa2, GluTR accumulates but is shifted toward membrane-associated inactive/sequestered form, uncoupling abundance from ALA flux
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GluTR physically associates with GSAAT and GBP in the ALA-synthesizing complex. 2. GSAAT deficiency, especially gsa2, increases total GluTR but reduces ALA synthesis, indicating that only a soluble complex-associated fraction is active. 3. Exogenous ALA promotes GluTR degradation in most backgrounds, while membrane-associated GluTR is stabilized in gsa2.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Engineering should consider stoichiometric balancing with GSAAT and GBP rather than increasing GluTR alone
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overaccumulation without matched GSAAT may lead to membrane sequestration instead of flux increase; subject to heme-dependent degradation/homeostatic control
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Cross-linking proteomics/MS; ProteomeXchange and jPOST: JPST001362
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although GluTR is a pathway enzyme and known rate-limiting factor, in this paper it serves mainly as an interacting/background comparator rather than the primary manipulated core gene.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Downstream step of the two-enzyme ALA-forming module
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts glutamate-1-semialdehyde to 5-aminolevulinic acid via aminomutase activity
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms the target product precursor pool for tetrapyrroles
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: ALA is the universal precursor of tetrapyrroles required for chlorophyll and heme biosynthesis; chlorophyll supports photosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Preferentially expressed in dry seeds, roots, and senescent leaves
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected in leaves; lower functional contribution than GSA2 in green leaves
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Chloroplast stroma; membrane-associated fraction detected when overexpressed in gsa2 background
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Short-day growth; continuous light with low, standard, or high light intensity
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA knockout mutant analysis; transgenic complementation/overexpression; enzyme activity assay; in vivo ALA synthesis assay; immunoblotting
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of GSA1 caused a mild reduction in GSAAT content/activity and modest chlorophyll decrease; 35S-driven GSA1 fully rescued gsa2 chlorophyll deficiency and ALA synthesis defects
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supports contribution of GSAAT to the rate-limiting ALA synthesis step, but GSA1 is not the predominant isoform in leaves
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GSA1 encodes one of two Arabidopsis GSAAT isoforms for ALA synthesis. 2. gsa1 mutants retain near-wild-type growth/chlorophyll, indicating a smaller role than GSA2 in green leaves. 3. Constitutive GSA1 expression fully complements gsa2, showing functional equivalence of the isoforms when sufficiently expressed.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Can be used to complement deficient ALA synthesis and chlorophyll formation; overexpression may restore flux when the dominant isoform is impaired
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No increase above wild-type ALA synthesis capacity in complementation lines despite overexpression; membrane-associated overproduced protein may represent inactive fraction
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Cross-linking proteomics/MS; ProteomeXchange and jPOST: JPST001362
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: GSA1 and GSA2 share 90% sequence identity and appear functionally equivalent biochemically, but differ in expression domain.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Downstream step of the two-enzyme ALA-forming module
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts glutamate-1-semialdehyde to 5-aminolevulinic acid via aminomutase activity
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms the target product precursor pool for tetrapyrroles
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: ALA is the universal precursor of tetrapyrroles required for chlorophyll and heme biosynthesis; chlorophyll supports photosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Predominantly expressed in green leaves and shoots; important in aerial green organs
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly relevant in leaves during light-induced ALA synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Short-day growth; continuous light with low, standard, or high light intensity; dark incubation with exogenous ALA
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA knockout mutant analysis; chlorophyll/heme quantification; GSAAT activity assay; in vivo ALA synthesis assay; subplastid fractionation; immunoblotting; BiFC; pull-down; cross-linking MS; MST-supported interaction framework
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: GSA2 loss strongly reduces GSAAT activity, ALA synthesis capacity, chlorophyll accumulation, and seedling growth; elevates GluTR/GBP accumulation and membrane association of GluTR
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Yes; strongly supported as contributing to the rate-limiting ALA synthesis capacity in planta, especially in leaves
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GSA2 is the predominant GSAAT isoform in photosynthetic tissues. 2. gsa2 causes major chlorophyll deficiency, reduced GSAAT activity, and reduced ALA synthesis despite elevated GluTR levels. 3. The data support that GSAAT2 content helps define active soluble GluTR and overall rate-limiting ALA synthesis capacity.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A prime target to sustain chlorophyll/tetrapyrrole biosynthesis in green tissues; maintaining GSA2 dosage/expression is important for photosynthetic performance
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Deficiency causes severe growth retardation and chlorophyll loss; excess GluTR accumulates in membrane-bound inactive/sequestered form rather than increasing ALA flux
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Cross-linking proteomics/MS; ProteomeXchange and jPOST: JPST001362
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Heme content was not significantly altered in gsa mutants despite chlorophyll reduction, indicating differential buffering of tetrapyrrole branches.
